AI.tldr: SeeGraph is a sparse-explanatory dynamic EEG-graph network for robust and interpretable dementia diagnosis from noisy EEG data, using node-guided sparse edge masks to reveal disease-relevant connections.
AI.motivation: Robust and interpretable dementia diagnosis from noisy, non-stationary EEG data is clinically essential but challenging due to noise, cross-site variability, and lack of transparency in existing methods.
AI.method: SeeGraph has four components: (1) dual-trajectory temporal encoder for dynamic EEG modeling, (2) topology-aware positional encoder using graph-spectral Laplacian, (3) node-guided sparse explanatory edge mask for connectivity sparsification, and (4) gated graph predictor. Trained with cross-entropy loss and sparsity regularization.
AI.result: Validated on public and in-house EEG cohorts with neurodegenerative dementia patients and healthy controls, showing effectiveness under both raw and noise-perturbed conditions. Sparse explanations highlight disease-relevant connections aligning with clinical findings.
AI.conclusion: SeeGraph provides noise-robust, interpretable dementia diagnosis with transparent explanations that reveal functional connectivity alterations, offering valuable cues for neurological evaluation.

title: Structured SIR: Efficient and Expressive Importance-Weighted Inference for High-Dimensional Image Registration
summary:
Image registration is an ill-posed dense vision task, where multiple solutions achieve similar loss values, motivating probabilistic inference. Variational inference has previously been employed to capture these distributions, however restrictive assumptions about the posterior form can lead to poor characterisation, overconfidence and low-quality samples. More flexible posteriors are typically bottlenecked by the complexity of high-dimensional covariance matrices required for dense 3D image registration.
  In this work, we present a memory and computationally efficient inference method, Structured SIR, that enables expressive, multi-modal, characterisation of uncertainty with high quality samples. We propose the use of a Sampled Importance Resampling (SIR) algorithm with a novel memory-efficient high-dimensional covariance parameterisation as the sum of a low-rank covariance and a sparse, spatially structured Cholesky precision factor. This structure enables capturing complex spatial correlations while remaining computationally tractable.
  We evaluate the efficacy of this approach in 3D dense image registration of brain MRI data, which is a very high-dimensional problem. We demonstrate that our proposed methods produces uncertainty estimates that are significantly better calibrated than those produced by variational methods, achieving equivalent or better accuracy. Crucially, we show that the model yields highly structured multi-modal posterior distributions, enable effective and efficient uncertainty quantification.

AI.tldr: Structured SIR: A memory-efficient probabilistic inference method for 3D image registration using sampled importance resampling with structured covariance parameterization to capture multi-modal uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Image registration is ill-posed with multiple valid solutions, requiring probabilistic inference. Existing variational methods with restrictive posterior assumptions lead to poor uncertainty characterization, overconfidence, and low-quality samples. Flexible posteriors are bottlenecked by high-dimensional covariance complexity in dense 3D registration.
AI.method: Proposes Structured SIR: Sampled Importance Resampling algorithm with novel memory-efficient high-dimensional covariance parameterization as sum of low-rank covariance and sparse, spatially structured Cholesky precision factor. This captures complex spatial correlations while remaining computationally tractable.
AI.result: Evaluation on 3D brain MRI registration shows significantly better calibrated uncertainty estimates than variational methods, with equivalent or better accuracy. Produces highly structured multi-modal posterior distributions enabling effective uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: Structured SIR enables expressive, multi-modal uncertainty characterization with high-quality samples for high-dimensional image registration, overcoming computational bottlenecks of traditional probabilistic methods while providing superior uncertainty calibration.

title: TeleDex: Accessible Dexterous Teleoperation
comment: For project website and videos, see https://www.orayyan.com/teledex
summary: Despite increasing dataset scale and model capacity, robot manipulation policies still struggle to generalize beyond their training distributions. As a result, deploying state-of-the-art policies in new environments, tasks, or robot embodiments often requires collecting additional demonstrations. Enabling this in real-world deployment settings requires tools that allow users to collect demonstrations quickly, affordably, and with minimal setup. We present TeleDex, an open-source system for intuitive teleoperation of dexterous hands and robotic manipulators using any readily available phone. The system streams low-latency 6-DoF wrist poses and articulated 21-DoF hand state estimates from the phone, which are retargeted to robot arms and multi-fingered hands without requiring external tracking infrastructure. TeleDex supports both a handheld phone-only mode and an optional 3D-printable hand-mounted interface for finger-level teleoperation. By lowering the hardware and setup barriers to dexterous teleoperation, TeleDex enables users to quickly collect demonstrations during deployment to support policy fine-tuning. We evaluate the system across simulation and real-world manipulation tasks, demonstrating its effectiveness as a unified scalable interface for robot teleoperation. All software and hardware designs, along with demonstration videos, are open-source and available at orayyan.com/teledex.

AI.tldr: TeleDex is an open-source teleoperation system that uses any smartphone to control dexterous robotic hands and manipulators, enabling quick, low-cost demonstration collection for policy fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Robot manipulation policies struggle to generalize beyond training distributions, requiring additional demonstrations for new environments, tasks, or robot embodiments. Current solutions need tools that allow quick, affordable demonstration collection with minimal setup in real-world deployment settings.
AI.method: TeleDex streams low-latency 6-DoF wrist poses and articulated 21-DoF hand state estimates from a phone, retargeting them to robot arms and multi-fingered hands without external tracking infrastructure. It supports both handheld phone-only mode and an optional 3D-printable hand-mounted interface for finger-level teleoperation.
AI.result: The system was evaluated across simulation and real-world manipulation tasks, demonstrating effectiveness as a unified scalable interface for robot teleoperation. It lowers hardware and setup barriers, enabling users to quickly collect demonstrations during deployment for policy fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: TeleDex provides an intuitive, accessible teleoperation solution using readily available smartphones, making dexterous demonstration collection practical for real-world deployment scenarios. All software, hardware designs, and demonstration videos are open-source.

title: What on Earth is AlphaEarth? Hierarchical structure and functional interpretability for global land cover
summary: Geospatial foundation models generate high-dimensional embeddings that achieve strong predictive performance, yet their internal organization remains obscure, limiting their scientific use. Recent interpretability studies relate Google AlphaEarth Foundations (GAEF) embeddings to continuous environmental variables, but it is still unclear whether the embedding space exhibits a functional or hierarchical organization, in which some dimensions act as specialized representations while others encode shared or broader geospatial structure. In this work, we propose a functional interpretability framework that reverse-engineers the role of embedding dimensions by characterizing their contribution to land cover structure from observed classification behavior. The approach combines large-scale experimentation with a structural analysis of embedding-class relationships based on feature importance patterns and progressive ablation. Our results show that embedding dimensions exhibit consistent and non-uniform functional behavior, allowing them to be categorized along a hierarchical functional spectrum: specialist dimensions associated with specific land cover classes, low- and mid-generalist dimensions capturing shared characteristics between classes, and highgeneralist dimensions reflecting broader environmental gradients. Critically, we find that accurate land cover classification (98% of baseline performance) can be achieved using as few as 2 to 12 of the 64 available dimensions, depending on the class. This demonstrates substantial redundancy in the embedding space and offers a pathway toward significant reductions in computational cost. Together, these findings reveal that AlphaEarth embeddings are not only physically informative, but also functionally organized into a hierarchical structure, providing practical guidance for dimension selection in operational classification tasks.

AI.tldr: GAEF embeddings show hierarchical functional organization with specialist, generalist dimensions; land cover classification can use only 2-12 of 64 dimensions with minimal performance loss.
AI.motivation: Geospatial foundation models produce high-dimensional embeddings with strong predictive performance, but their internal organization remains unclear, limiting scientific utility. While recent studies relate embeddings to environmental variables, it's unknown whether embedding space has functional/hierarchical organization with specialized vs. shared representations.
AI.method: Proposed functional interpretability framework that reverse-engineers embedding dimension roles by analyzing their contribution to land cover structure from classification behavior. Combines large-scale experimentation with structural analysis of embedding-class relationships using feature importance patterns and progressive ablation.
AI.result: Embedding dimensions show consistent non-uniform functional behavior, categorized hierarchically: specialist dimensions for specific land cover classes, low/mid-generalist dimensions for shared class characteristics, and high-generalist dimensions for broader environmental gradients. Land cover classification achieves 98% baseline performance using only 2-12 of 64 dimensions (class-dependent), revealing substantial redundancy.
AI.conclusion: AlphaEarth embeddings are not only physically informative but functionally organized into hierarchical structure. This provides practical guidance for dimension selection in operational classification tasks and demonstrates pathway for significant computational cost reduction through dimension pruning.

title: Script-to-Slide Grounding: Grounding Script Sentences to Slide Objects for Automatic Instructional Video Generation
comment: The 21st International Conference on E-Service and Knowledge Management (ESKM 2025-Winter)
summary: While slide-based videos augmented with visual effects are widely utilized in education and research presentations, the video editing process -- particularly applying visual effects to ground spoken content to slide objects -- remains highly labor-intensive. This study aims to develop a system that automatically generates such instructional videos from slides and corresponding scripts. As a foundational step, this paper proposes and formulates Script-to-Slide Grounding (S2SG), defined as the task of grounding script sentences to their corresponding slide objects. Furthermore, as an initial step, we propose ``Text-S2SG,'' a method that utilizes a large language model (LLM) to perform this grounding task for text objects. Our experiments demonstrate that the proposed method achieves high performance (F1-score: 0.924). The contribution of this work is the formalization of a previously implicit slide-based video editing process into a computable task, thereby paving the way for its automation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Script-to-Slide Grounding (S2SG) as a formal task for automating slide-based video creation, and introduces Text-S2SG, an LLM-based method that achieves high performance (F1: 0.924) for grounding text objects.
AI.motivation: Creating instructional videos from slides with visual effects synchronized to spoken content is labor-intensive. The authors aim to automate this process by first solving the fundamental problem of grounding script sentences to slide objects.
AI.method: The paper formalizes Script-to-Slide Grounding (S2SG) as a computational task. As an initial approach, they propose Text-S2SG, which uses a large language model (LLM) to ground script sentences to text objects in slides.
AI.result: The Text-S2SG method achieves high performance with an F1-score of 0.924 in grounding script sentences to slide text objects.
AI.conclusion: The work formalizes an implicit video editing process into a computable task (S2SG), paving the way for automation of slide-based video creation. The proposed Text-S2SG method shows promising results as a foundational step.

title: AI Scientist via Synthetic Task Scaling
summary: With the advent of AI agents, automatic scientific discovery has become a tenable goal. Many recent works scaffold agentic systems that can perform machine learning research, but don't offer a principled way to train such agents -- and current LLMs often generate plausible-looking but ineffective ideas. To make progress on training agents that can learn from doing, we provide a novel synthetic environment generation pipeline targeting machine learning agents. Our pipeline automatically synthesizes machine learning challenges compatible with the SWE-agent framework, covering topic sampling, dataset proposal, and code generation. The resulting synthetic tasks are 1) grounded in real machine learning datasets, because the proposed datasets are verified against the Huggingface API and are 2) verified for higher quality with a self-debugging loop. To validate the effectiveness of our synthetic tasks, we tackle MLGym, a benchmark for machine learning tasks. From the synthetic tasks, we sample trajectories from a teacher model (GPT-5), then use the trajectories to train a student model (Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B). The student models trained with our synthetic tasks achieve improved performance on MLGym, raising the AUP metric by 9% for Qwen3-4B and 12% for Qwen3-8B.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a synthetic environment generation pipeline for training machine learning agents, which automatically creates ML challenges compatible with SWE-agent framework and improves student model performance on the MLGym benchmark.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents for scientific discovery lack principled training methods, and LLMs often generate plausible but ineffective ideas. There's a need for better ways to train agents that can learn from doing machine learning research.
AI.method: Developed a synthetic environment generation pipeline that: 1) samples ML topics, 2) proposes datasets verified against Huggingface API, 3) generates code, and 4) uses a self-debugging loop for quality verification. Used GPT-5 as teacher model to generate trajectories from synthetic tasks, then trained student models (Qwen3-4B and Qwen3-8B) on these trajectories.
AI.result: Student models trained with synthetic tasks showed improved performance on MLGym benchmark: Qwen3-4B achieved 9% AUP improvement and Qwen3-8B achieved 12% AUP improvement.
AI.conclusion: The synthetic environment generation pipeline effectively trains machine learning agents, demonstrating that synthetic tasks grounded in real datasets can significantly improve agent performance on ML research benchmarks.

title: EEG-Based Brain-LLM Interface for Human Preference Aligned Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are becoming an increasingly important component of human--computer interaction, enabling users to coordinate a wide range of intelligent agents through natural language. While language-based interfaces are powerful and flexible, they implicitly assume that users can reliably produce explicit linguistic input, an assumption that may not hold for users with speech or motor impairments, e.g., Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). In this work, we investigate whether neural signals can be used as an alternative input to LLMs, particularly to support those socially marginalized or underserved users. We build a simple brain-LLM interface, which uses EEG signals to guide image generation models at test time. Specifically, we first train a classifier to estimate user satisfaction from EEG signals. Its predictions are then incorporated into a test-time scaling (TTS) framework that dynamically adapts model inference using neural feedback collected during user evaluation. The experiments show that EEG can predict user satisfaction, suggesting that neural activity carries information on real-time preference inference. These findings provide a first step toward integrating neural feedback into adaptive language-model inference, and hopefully open up new possibilities for future research on adaptive LLM interaction.

AI.tldr: Researchers develop a brain-LLM interface using EEG signals to predict user satisfaction and adapt image generation models in real-time, aiming to support users with speech/motor impairments.
AI.motivation: Current LLM interfaces assume users can reliably produce explicit linguistic input, which excludes users with speech/motor impairments like ALS. The paper investigates whether neural signals can serve as alternative input to LLMs to support marginalized users.
AI.method: Built a brain-LLM interface using EEG signals. First trained a classifier to estimate user satisfaction from EEG signals, then incorporated predictions into a test-time scaling (TTS) framework that dynamically adapts model inference using neural feedback during user evaluation.
AI.result: Experiments show EEG can predict user satisfaction, indicating neural activity carries information about real-time preference inference. The brain-LLM interface successfully guides image generation models at test time.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a first step toward integrating neural feedback into adaptive language-model inference, opening new possibilities for adaptive LLM interaction, particularly for users with speech/motor impairments.

title: Deep Learning-Based Airway Segmentation in Systemic Lupus Erythematosus Patients with Interstitial Lung Disease (SLE-ILD): A Comparative High-Resolution CT Analysis
summary: To characterize lobar and segmental airway volume differences between systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) patients with interstitial lung disease (ILD) and those without ILD (non-ILD) using a deep learning-based approach on non-contrast chest high-resolution CT (HRCT). Methods: A retrospective analysis was conducted on 106 SLE patients (27 SLE-ILD, 79 SLE-non-ILD) who underwent HRCT. A customized deep learning framework based on the U-Net architecture was developed to automatically segment airway structures at the lobar and segmental levels via HRCT. Volumetric measurements of lung lobes and segments derived from the segmentations were statistically compared between the two groups using two-sample t-tests (significance threshold: p < 0.05). Results: At lobar level, significant airway volume enlargement in SLE-ILD patients was observed in the right upper lobe (p=0.009) and left upper lobe (p=0.039) compared to SLE-non-ILD. At the segmental level, significant differences were found in segments including R1 (p=0.016), R3 (p<0.001), and L3 (p=0.038), with the most marked changes in the upper lung zones, while lower zones showed non-significant trends. Conclusion: Our study demonstrates that an automated deep learning-based approach can effectively quantify airway volumes on HRCT scans and reveal significant, region-specific airway dilation in patients with SLE-ILD compared to those without ILD. The pattern of involvement, predominantly affecting the upper lobes and specific segments, highlights a distinct topographic phenotype of SLE-ILD and implicates airway structural alterations as a potential biomarker for disease presence. This AI-powered quantitative imaging biomarker holds promise for enhancing the early detection and monitoring of ILD in the SLE population, ultimately contributing to more personalized patient management.

AI.tldr: Deep learning-based airway segmentation on HRCT reveals significant upper lobe airway dilation in SLE patients with ILD compared to those without ILD, suggesting airway volume as a potential biomarker for SLE-ILD detection.
AI.motivation: To characterize lobar and segmental airway volume differences between SLE patients with and without ILD using automated deep learning analysis of chest HRCT scans, aiming to identify quantitative imaging biomarkers for SLE-ILD.
AI.method: Retrospective analysis of 106 SLE patients (27 with ILD, 79 without) using a customized U-Net deep learning framework to automatically segment airway structures at lobar and segmental levels from HRCT scans. Statistical comparison of volumetric measurements between groups using two-sample t-tests.
AI.result: Significant airway volume enlargement in SLE-ILD patients was found in right upper lobe (p=0.009) and left upper lobe (p=0.039), and at segmental level in R1 (p=0.016), R3 (p<0.001), and L3 (p=0.038). Upper lung zones showed most marked changes while lower zones showed non-significant trends.
AI.conclusion: Automated deep learning can quantify airway volumes and reveal region-specific airway dilation in SLE-ILD, predominantly affecting upper lobes and specific segments. This AI-powered quantitative imaging biomarker shows promise for early detection and monitoring of ILD in SLE patients.

title: SLowRL: Safe Low-Rank Adaptation Reinforcement Learning for Locomotion
summary: Sim-to-real transfer of locomotion policies often leads to performance degradation due to the inevitable sim-to-real gap. Naively fine-tuning these policies directly on hardware is problematic, as it poses risks of mechanical failure and suffers from high sample inefficiency. In this paper, we address the challenge of safely and efficiently fine-tuning reinforcement learning (RL) policies for dynamic locomotion tasks. Specifically, we focus on fine-tuning policies learned in simulation directly on hardware, while explicitly enforcing safety constraints. In doing so, we introduce SLowRL, a framework that combines Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with training-time safety enforcement via a recovery policy. We evaluate our method both in simulation and on a real Unitree Go2 quadruped robot for jump and trot tasks. Experimental results show that our method achieves a $46.5\%$ reduction in fine-tuning time and near-zero safety violations compared to standard proximal policy optimization (PPO) baselines. Notably, we find that a rank-1 adaptation alone is sufficient to recover pre-trained performance in the real world, while maintaining stable and safe real-world fine-tuning. These results demonstrate the practicality of safe, efficient fine-tuning for dynamic real-world robotic applications.

AI.tldr: SLowRL framework combines Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with safety enforcement for efficient, safe sim-to-real fine-tuning of locomotion policies, reducing fine-tuning time by 46.5% with near-zero safety violations.
AI.motivation: Sim-to-real transfer often degrades performance due to reality gap, and naive hardware fine-tuning risks mechanical failure and suffers from sample inefficiency. Need safe, efficient fine-tuning for dynamic locomotion tasks.
AI.method: SLowRL combines Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) with training-time safety enforcement via a recovery policy. Fine-tunes policies learned in simulation directly on hardware while explicitly enforcing safety constraints.
AI.result: 46.5% reduction in fine-tuning time and near-zero safety violations compared to standard PPO baselines. Rank-1 adaptation alone recovers pre-trained performance in real world while maintaining stable, safe fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: SLowRL demonstrates practicality of safe, efficient fine-tuning for dynamic real-world robotic applications, enabling stable sim-to-real transfer with minimal safety risks and reduced training time.

title: HoloByte: Continuous Hyperspherical Distillation for Tokenizer-Free Modeling
summary: Sequence modeling universally relies on discrete subword tokenization to circumvent the $\mathcal{O}(N^2)$ computational intractability of native byte-level attention. However, this heuristic quantization imposes artificial morphological boundaries, enforces vocabulary dependence, and fractures the continuity of the optimization landscape. To resolve this dichotomy, we introduce \textbf{HoloByte}: a strictly tokenizer-free framework utilizing Continuous Hyperspherical Distillation. HoloByte partitions discrete byte sequences into fixed-capacity chunks and projects them into a continuous, strictly bounded hyperspherical manifold via an invertible, dimension-preserving orthogonal rotation operator. This spatial superposition allows a macroscopic transformer to operate exclusively on compressed continuous representations, formally reducing the exact attention time complexity from $\mathcal{O}(N^2D)$ to $\mathcal{O}\left( \frac{N^2}{W^2}D + ND^2 \right)$. A localized causal micro-decoder subsequently unbinds these representations to compute exact byte-level distributions. To govern this continuous trajectory, we propose a dual-objective formulation incorporating a mathematically precise Holographic Latent Mean Squared Error, which strictly bounds the gradient and guarantees asymptotic stability. Theoretically, we derive the minimal embedding dimension $D = Ω(W \ln |\mathcal{V}|)$ required to ensure error-free discrete recovery from the continuous manifold. Empirically, under strictly matched parameter constraints, HoloByte is systematically outperforming a comparable discrete Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) baseline. These results establish Continuous Hyperspherical Distillation as a mathematically rigorous and computationally tractable foundation for vocabulary-invariant sequence modeling. The code is available at https://github.com/VladimerKhasia/HoloByte

AI.tldr: HoloByte is a tokenizer-free framework using continuous hyperspherical distillation to bypass discrete subword tokenization, reducing attention complexity while maintaining exact byte-level recovery.
AI.motivation: Discrete subword tokenization imposes artificial morphological boundaries, enforces vocabulary dependence, and fractures optimization landscape continuity. Current approaches have O(N²) computational intractability at byte level.
AI.method: Partitions byte sequences into fixed-capacity chunks, projects them into continuous hyperspherical manifold via invertible orthogonal rotation. Uses macroscopic transformer on compressed continuous representations with micro-decoder for byte-level distributions. Dual-objective formulation with Holographic Latent MSE for gradient bounding and stability.
AI.result: Theoretically derives minimal embedding dimension D = Ω(W ln|V|) for error-free discrete recovery. Empirically outperforms BPE baseline under matched parameter constraints. Reduces exact attention complexity from O(N²D) to O(N²/W² D + ND²).
AI.conclusion: Continuous Hyperspherical Distillation provides mathematically rigorous, computationally tractable foundation for vocabulary-invariant sequence modeling, establishing tokenizer-free approach as viable alternative to discrete tokenization.

title: Look Where It Matters: High-Resolution Crops Retrieval for Efficient VLMs
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) typically process images at a native high-resolution, forcing a trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency: high-resolution inputs capture fine details but incur significant computational costs, while low-resolution inputs advocate for efficiency, they potentially miss critical visual information, like small text. We present AwaRes, a spatial-on-demand framework that resolves this accuracy-efficiency trade-off by operating on a low-resolution global view and using tool-calling to retrieve only high-resolution segments needed for a given query. We construct supervised data automatically: a judge compares low- vs.\ high-resolution answers to label whether cropping is needed, and an oracle grounding model localizes the evidence for the correct answer, which we map to a discrete crop set to form multi-turn tool-use trajectories. We train our framework with cold-start SFT followed by multi-turn GRPO with a composite reward that combines semantic answer correctness with explicit crop-cost penalties. Project page: https://nimrodshabtay.github.io/AwaRes

AI.tldr: AwaRes is a spatial-on-demand framework that uses tool-calling to retrieve only necessary high-resolution image segments, balancing accuracy and efficiency in vision-language models.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs face an accuracy-efficiency trade-off: high-resolution inputs capture fine details but are computationally expensive, while low-resolution inputs are efficient but miss critical visual information like small text.
AI.method: Uses low-resolution global view with tool-calling to retrieve only needed high-resolution segments. Automatically constructs supervised data: judge compares low vs high-resolution answers to determine cropping needs, oracle grounding model localizes evidence, maps to discrete crop set for multi-turn tool-use trajectories. Training involves cold-start SFT followed by multi-turn GRPO with composite reward combining semantic correctness and crop-cost penalties.
AI.result: The framework resolves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off by dynamically retrieving only necessary high-resolution segments based on query needs.
AI.conclusion: AwaRes provides an effective spatial-on-demand approach that maintains accuracy while improving computational efficiency through selective high-resolution processing.

title: Draft-and-Prune: Improving the Reliability of Auto-formalization for Logical Reasoning
summary: Auto-formalization (AF) translates natural-language reasoning problems into solver-executable programs, enabling symbolic solvers to perform sound logical deduction. In practice, however, AF pipelines are currently brittle: programs may fail to execute, or execute but encode incorrect semantics. While prior work largely mitigates syntactic failures via repairs based on solver feedback, reducing semantics failures remains a major bottleneck. We propose Draft-and-Prune (D&P), an inference-time framework that improves AF-based logical reasoning via diversity and verification. D&P first drafts multiple natural-language plans and conditions program generation on them. It further prunes executable but contradictory or ambiguous formalizations, and aggregates predictions from surviving paths via majority voting. Across four representative benchmarks (AR-LSAT, ProofWriter, PrOntoQA, LogicalDeduction), D&P substantially strengthens AF-based reasoning without extra supervision. On AR-LSAT, in the AF-only setting, D&P achieves 78.43% accuracy with GPT-4 and 78.00% accuracy with GPT-4o, significantly outperforming the strongest AF baselines MAD-LOGIC and CLOVER. D&P then attains near-ceiling performance on the other benchmarks, including 100% on PrOntoQA and LogicalDeduction.

AI.tldr: Draft-and-Prune (D&P) improves auto-formalization for logical reasoning by generating multiple natural-language plans, pruning contradictory formalizations, and aggregating predictions via majority voting.
AI.motivation: Current auto-formalization pipelines are brittle - programs may fail to execute or encode incorrect semantics. While syntactic failures can be mitigated, reducing semantic failures remains a major bottleneck in AF-based logical reasoning.
AI.method: Draft-and-Prune framework: 1) Drafts multiple natural-language plans and conditions program generation on them, 2) Prunes executable but contradictory or ambiguous formalizations, 3) Aggregates predictions from surviving paths via majority voting.
AI.result: D&P substantially improves AF-based reasoning across four benchmarks: AR-LSAT (78.43% with GPT-4, 78.00% with GPT-4o), near-ceiling performance on ProofWriter, PrOntoQA (100%), and LogicalDeduction (100%), outperforming strong baselines like MAD-LOGIC and CLOVER.
AI.conclusion: Draft-and-Prune effectively addresses semantic failures in auto-formalization through diversity and verification, achieving strong performance without extra supervision and significantly advancing AF-based logical reasoning capabilities.

title: CRB-Based Resource Allocation in Multi-User Uplink Transmissions
summary: In this work, we study the design of receivers for uplink multi-user systems, aiming to estimate both the channel and the transmitted symbols. We consider two estimation strategies: (i) a joint estimation approach, where the channel and symbols are estimated simultaneously, and (ii) a sequential estimation approach, where the channel is first estimated and then used for symbol detection. For both strategies, we derive the Cramér-Rao Bound (CRB) for symbol estimation to characterize fundamental performance limits. When efficient receivers achieving the CRB exist, these bounds provide accurate lower bounds on the mutual information. In general, however, such receivers may not be available, and we instead use these same CRB-based metrics as practical proxies for achievable throughput. Leveraging tools from random matrix theory (RMT), we analyze the asymptotic behavior of these lower bounds under various asymptotic regimes for both estimation strategies. This analysis enables the derivation of generic power allocation guidelines that asymptotically maximize the proxy metrics. Simulation results confirm the accuracy of the asymptotic expressions and their effectiveness in guiding resource allocation decisions.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes receiver design for uplink multi-user systems, comparing joint vs. sequential channel/symbol estimation strategies and deriving Cramér-Rao Bounds to characterize fundamental performance limits.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to understand fundamental performance limits and design efficient receivers for uplink multi-user systems that need to estimate both channel conditions and transmitted symbols, which is crucial for practical communication systems.
AI.method: The paper considers two estimation strategies: (1) joint estimation of channel and symbols simultaneously, and (2) sequential estimation where channel is estimated first then used for symbol detection. It derives Cramér-Rao Bounds for both approaches and uses random matrix theory to analyze asymptotic behavior under various regimes.
AI.result: The analysis provides asymptotic expressions for performance bounds and enables derivation of generic power allocation guidelines that asymptotically maximize proxy metrics for achievable throughput. Simulation results confirm the accuracy of these asymptotic expressions.
AI.conclusion: The CRB-based metrics serve as practical proxies for achievable throughput when efficient receivers are unavailable, and the asymptotic analysis provides effective guidelines for resource allocation decisions in uplink multi-user systems.

title: DAPS++: Rethinking Diffusion Inverse Problems with Decoupled Posterior Annealing
summary: From a Bayesian perspective, score-based diffusion solves inverse problems through joint inference, embedding the likelihood with the prior to guide the sampling process. However, this formulation fails to explain its practical behavior: the prior offers limited guidance, while reconstruction is largely driven by the measurement-consistency term, leading to an inference process that is effectively decoupled from the diffusion dynamics. To clarify this structure, we reinterpret the role of diffusion in inverse problem solving as an initialization stage within an expectation--maximization (EM)--style framework, where the diffusion stage and the data-driven refinement are fully decoupled. We introduce \textbf{DAPS++}, which allows the likelihood term to guide inference more directly while maintaining numerical stability and providing insight into why unified diffusion trajectories remain effective in practice. By requiring fewer function evaluations (NFEs) and measurement-optimization steps, \textbf{DAPS++} achieves high computational efficiency and robust reconstruction performance across diverse image restoration tasks.

AI.tldr: DAPS++ reinterprets diffusion-based inverse problem solving as an EM-style framework with decoupled diffusion initialization and data-driven refinement, achieving computational efficiency and robust performance.
AI.motivation: Current Bayesian interpretation of score-based diffusion for inverse problems fails to explain practical behavior - the prior offers limited guidance while reconstruction is largely driven by measurement consistency, effectively decoupling inference from diffusion dynamics.
AI.method: Reinterpret diffusion as initialization stage within EM-style framework, fully decoupling diffusion stage and data-driven refinement. DAPS++ allows likelihood term to guide inference more directly while maintaining numerical stability.
AI.result: DAPS++ achieves high computational efficiency with fewer function evaluations and measurement-optimization steps, while providing robust reconstruction performance across diverse image restoration tasks.
AI.conclusion: The EM-style framework clarifies why unified diffusion trajectories remain effective in practice, offering a more accurate interpretation of diffusion-based inverse problem solving with improved computational efficiency.

title: Shielded Reinforcement Learning Under Dynamic Temporal Logic Constraints
comment: 7 pages, 3 figures, 2026 IEEE American Control Conference (ACC)
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has shown promise in various robotics applications, yet its deployment on real systems is still limited due to safety and operational constraints. The safe RL field has gained considerable attention in recent years, which focuses on imposing safety constraints throughout the learning process. However, real systems often require more complex constraints than just safety, such as periodic recharging or time-bounded visits to specific regions. Imposing such spatio-temporal tasks during learning still remains a challenge. Signal Temporal Logic (STL) is a formal language for specifying temporal properties of real-valued signals and provides a way to express such complex tasks. In this paper, we propose a framework that leverages sequential control barrier functions and model-free RL to ensure that the given STL tasks are satisfied throughout the learning process. Our method extends beyond traditional safety constraints by enforcing rich STL specifications, which can involve visits to dynamic targets with unknown trajectories. We also demonstrate the effectiveness of our framework through various simulations.

AI.tldr: A framework combining sequential control barrier functions with model-free RL to enforce Signal Temporal Logic (STL) specifications during learning, enabling complex spatio-temporal tasks beyond traditional safety constraints.
AI.motivation: Real-world robotics applications require more than just safety constraints - they need complex spatio-temporal tasks like periodic recharging or time-bounded visits to specific regions. Current safe RL methods struggle with these complex constraints, and STL provides a formal way to express such requirements, but imposing them during learning remains challenging.
AI.method: The proposed framework integrates sequential control barrier functions with model-free reinforcement learning. This combination ensures that given STL specifications are satisfied throughout the learning process, even for tasks involving visits to dynamic targets with unknown trajectories.
AI.result: The framework successfully enforces rich STL specifications during RL learning, extending beyond traditional safety constraints. The effectiveness is demonstrated through various simulations showing the method's capability to handle complex spatio-temporal tasks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework addresses the challenge of imposing complex spatio-temporal constraints during RL learning by leveraging STL specifications and sequential control barrier functions, enabling safer and more practical deployment of RL in real robotics systems.

title: MHPO: Modulated Hazard-aware Policy Optimization for Stable Reinforcement Learning
summary: Regulating the importance ratio is critical for the training stability of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) based frameworks. However, prevailing ratio control methods, such as hard clipping, suffer from non-differentiable boundaries and vanishing gradient regions, failing to maintain gradient fidelity. Furthermore, these methods lack a hazard-aware mechanism to adaptively suppress extreme deviations, leaving the optimization process vulnerable to abrupt policy shifts. To address these challenges, we propose Modulated Hazard-aware Policy Optimization (MHPO), a novel framework designed for robust and stable reinforcement learning. The proposed MHPO introduces a Log-Fidelity Modulator (LFM) to map unbounded importance ratios into a bounded, differentiable domain. This mechanism effectively prevents high-variance outlier tokens from destabilizing the loss landscape while ensuring global gradient stability. Complementarily, a Decoupled Hazard Penalty (DHP) integrates cumulative hazard functions from survival analysis to independently regulate positive and negative policy shifts. By shaping the optimization landscape with hazard-aware penalties, the proposed MHPO achieves fine-grained regulation of asymmetric policy shifts simultaneously mitigating mode collapse from over-expansion and preventing policy erosion from catastrophic contraction within a stabilized trust region. Extensive evaluations on diverse reasoning benchmarks across both text-based and vision-language tasks demonstrate that MHPO consistently outperforms existing methods, achieving superior performance while significantly enhancing training stability.

AI.tldr: MHPO introduces Log-Fidelity Modulator and Decoupled Hazard Penalty to stabilize RL training by bounding importance ratios and adaptively regulating policy shifts, outperforming existing methods on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current ratio control methods like hard clipping have issues with non-differentiable boundaries, vanishing gradients, and lack hazard-aware mechanisms to suppress extreme policy deviations, making optimization vulnerable to abrupt policy shifts.
AI.method: MHPO uses two key components: 1) Log-Fidelity Modulator (LFM) maps unbounded importance ratios to bounded differentiable domain to prevent high-variance outliers; 2) Decoupled Hazard Penalty (DHP) integrates survival analysis hazard functions to independently regulate positive/negative policy shifts, preventing mode collapse and policy erosion.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on diverse reasoning benchmarks across text-based and vision-language tasks show MHPO consistently outperforms existing methods, achieving superior performance while significantly enhancing training stability.
AI.conclusion: MHPO provides a robust framework for stable reinforcement learning through differentiable ratio bounding and hazard-aware policy regulation, effectively addressing limitations of previous ratio control methods in GRPO-based frameworks.

title: AgriChat: A Multimodal Large Language Model for Agriculture Image Understanding
summary: The deployment of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in agriculture is currently stalled by a critical trade-off: the existing literature lacks the large-scale agricultural datasets required for robust model development and evaluation, while current state-of-the-art models lack the verified domain expertise necessary to reason across diverse taxonomies. To address these challenges, we propose the Vision-to-Verified-Knowledge (V2VK) pipeline, a novel generative AI-driven annotation framework that integrates visual captioning with web-augmented scientific retrieval to autonomously generate the AgriMM benchmark, effectively eliminating biological hallucinations by grounding training data in verified phytopathological literature. The AgriMM benchmark contains over 3,000 agricultural classes and more than 607k VQAs spanning multiple tasks, including fine-grained plant species identification, plant disease symptom recognition, crop counting, and ripeness assessment. Leveraging this verifiable data, we present AgriChat, a specialized MLLM that presents broad knowledge across thousands of agricultural classes and provides detailed agricultural assessments with extensive explanations. Extensive evaluation across diverse tasks, datasets, and evaluation conditions reveals both the capabilities and limitations of current agricultural MLLMs, while demonstrating AgriChat's superior performance over other open-source models, including internal and external benchmarks. The results validate that preserving visual detail combined with web-verified knowledge constitutes a reliable pathway toward robust and trustworthy agricultural AI. The code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/boudiafA/AgriChat .

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgriMM, a large-scale agricultural benchmark generated via V2VK pipeline, and AgriChat, a specialized MLLM that outperforms other models on diverse agricultural tasks.
AI.motivation: Current agricultural MLLM deployment is hindered by two key issues: lack of large-scale agricultural datasets for robust development/evaluation, and absence of verified domain expertise in existing models for reasoning across diverse taxonomies.
AI.method: Proposes V2VK (Vision-to-Verified-Knowledge) pipeline - a generative AI-driven annotation framework that integrates visual captioning with web-augmented scientific retrieval to autonomously generate the AgriMM benchmark, grounding training data in verified phytopathological literature to eliminate biological hallucinations.
AI.result: Created AgriMM benchmark with over 3,000 agricultural classes and 607k VQAs spanning multiple tasks. Developed AgriChat MLLM that demonstrates superior performance over other open-source models across diverse tasks, datasets, and evaluation conditions.
AI.conclusion: Preserving visual detail combined with web-verified knowledge provides a reliable pathway toward robust and trustworthy agricultural AI. The approach effectively addresses the trade-off between dataset scale and domain expertise verification.

title: Graph-Native Cognitive Memory for AI Agents: Formal Belief Revision Semantics for Versioned Memory Architectures
summary: While individual components for AI agent memory exist in prior systems, their architectural synthesis and formal grounding remain underexplored. We present Kumiho, a graph-native cognitive memory architecture grounded in formal belief revision semantics. The structural primitives required for cognitive memory -- immutable revisions, mutable tag pointers, typed dependency edges, URI-based addressing -- are identical to those required for managing agent-produced work as versionable assets, enabling a unified graph-native architecture that serves both purposes. The central formal contribution is a correspondence between the AGM belief revision framework and the operational semantics of a property graph memory system, proving satisfaction of the basic AGM postulates (K*2--K*6) and Hansson's belief base postulates (Relevance, Core-Retainment). The architecture implements a dual-store model (Redis working memory, Neo4j long-term graph) with hybrid fulltext and vector retrieval. On LoCoMo (token-level F1), Kumiho achieves 0.565 overall F1 (n=1,986) including 97.5% adversarial refusal accuracy. On LoCoMo-Plus, a Level-2 cognitive memory benchmark testing implicit constraint recall, Kumiho achieves 93.3% judge accuracy (n=401); independent reproduction by the benchmark authors yielded results in the mid-80% range, still substantially outperforming all published baselines (best: Gemini 2.5 Pro, 45.7%). Three architectural innovations drive the results: prospective indexing (LLM-generated future-scenario implications indexed at write time), event extraction (structured causal events preserved in summaries), and client-side LLM reranking. The architecture is model-decoupled: switching the answer model from GPT-4o-mini (~88%) to GPT-4o (93.3%) improves end-to-end accuracy without pipeline changes, at a total evaluation cost of ~$14 for 401 entries.

AI.tldr: Kumiho is a graph-native cognitive memory architecture for AI agents that unifies memory management and asset versioning using formal belief revision semantics, achieving state-of-the-art performance on cognitive memory benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Prior AI agent memory systems lack architectural synthesis and formal grounding. Individual components exist but their integration and theoretical foundation remain underexplored, creating a need for a unified, formally-grounded memory architecture.
AI.method: A graph-native cognitive memory architecture using formal belief revision semantics (AGM framework). Features dual-store model (Redis working memory, Neo4j long-term graph), hybrid fulltext/vector retrieval, prospective indexing (LLM-generated future implications), event extraction (structured causal events), and client-side LLM reranking.
AI.result: Achieves 0.565 overall F1 on LoCoMo (97.5% adversarial refusal accuracy) and 93.3% judge accuracy on LoCoMo-Plus (implicit constraint recall benchmark), substantially outperforming all published baselines (best baseline: Gemini 2.5 Pro at 45.7%).
AI.conclusion: Kumiho demonstrates that a unified graph-native architecture grounded in formal belief revision can effectively serve both cognitive memory and asset management needs while achieving state-of-the-art performance on cognitive memory benchmarks.

title: Physical Layer Security for FAS-Aided Short-Packet Systems: A Variable Block-Correlation Approach
summary: This paper presents a comprehensive physical layer security (PLS) framework for fluid antenna system (FAS)-aided short-packet communications under the variable block-correlation model (VBCM). We consider a downlink wiretap scenario in which a base station transmits confidential short packets to a legitimate receiver user (RU) in the presence of an eavesdropper user (EU), where both the RU and EU are equipped with fluid antennas. Unlike existing FAS security analyses that rely on constant block-correlation models or infinite-blocklength assumptions, we incorporate the VBCM to accurately capture the non-uniform spatial correlation structure inherent in practical FAS deployments. By employing a piecewise linear approximation of the decoding error probability and Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature, we derive closed-form and asymptotic expressions for the average achievable secrecy throughput (AAST). We further prove that the AAST is monotonically non-decreasing in the number of RU ports, which reduces the three-dimensional joint optimization of transmit power, blocklength, and port number to a two-dimensional grid search (GS). Numerical results demonstrate that the FAS-aided system achieves up to an order-of-magnitude secrecy throughput improvement over conventional fixed-position antenna systems, and reveal that blocklength selection is the most critical design parameter in the joint optimization.

AI.tldr: FAS-aided short-packet PLS framework with VBCM achieves order-of-magnitude secrecy throughput gains over fixed antennas, with blocklength being the most critical optimization parameter.
AI.motivation: Existing FAS security analyses use unrealistic constant correlation models or infinite-blocklength assumptions, failing to capture practical non-uniform spatial correlation in FAS deployments for short-packet communications.
AI.method: Develop comprehensive PLS framework for FAS-aided short-packet communications using VBCM, derive closed-form/asymptotic AAST expressions via piecewise linear approximation and Gauss-Chebyshev quadrature, prove AAST monotonicity to reduce optimization complexity.
AI.result: FAS system achieves up to order-of-magnitude secrecy throughput improvement over fixed antennas; AAST is monotonically non-decreasing in RU ports; blocklength selection is most critical parameter in joint optimization.
AI.conclusion: The proposed VBCM-based FAS framework significantly enhances short-packet security, with practical optimization reduced to 2D grid search, demonstrating FAS's superiority over conventional antenna systems for secure communications.

title: SLAM Adversarial Lab: An Extensible Framework for Visual SLAM Robustness Evaluation under Adverse Conditions
summary: We present SAL (SLAM Adversarial Lab), a modular framework for evaluating visual SLAM systems under adversarial conditions such as fog and rain. SAL represents each adversarial condition as a perturbation that transforms an existing dataset into an adversarial dataset. When transforming a dataset, SAL supports severity levels using easily-interpretable real-world units such as meters for fog visibility. SAL's extensible architecture decouples datasets, perturbations, and SLAM algorithms through common interfaces, so users can add new components without rewriting integration code. Moreover, SAL includes a search procedure that finds the severity level of a perturbation at which a SLAM system fails. To showcase the capabilities of SAL, our evaluation integrates seven SLAM algorithms and evaluates them across three datasets under weather, camera, and video transport perturbations.

AI.tldr: SAL is a modular framework for evaluating visual SLAM systems under adversarial conditions like fog and rain by transforming datasets with severity-controlled perturbations.
AI.motivation: There's a need to systematically evaluate how visual SLAM systems perform under challenging real-world conditions (weather, camera issues, video transport problems) to understand their robustness and failure points.
AI.method: SAL uses a modular architecture with common interfaces that decouple datasets, perturbations, and SLAM algorithms. It represents adversarial conditions as perturbations that transform existing datasets, supports severity levels in real-world units, and includes a search procedure to find failure points.
AI.result: The framework successfully integrates seven SLAM algorithms and evaluates them across three datasets under various perturbations including weather, camera, and video transport conditions.
AI.conclusion: SAL provides an extensible, systematic approach for evaluating SLAM robustness under adversarial conditions, enabling researchers to better understand and improve SLAM system performance in challenging real-world scenarios.

title: Integrating Explainable Machine Learning and Mixed-Integer Optimization for Personalized Sleep Quality Intervention
summary: Sleep quality is influenced by a complex interplay of behavioral, environmental, and psychosocial factors, yet most computational studies focus mainly on predictive risk identification rather than actionable intervention design. Although machine learning models can accurately predict subjective sleep outcomes, they rarely translate predictive insights into practical intervention strategies. To address this gap, we propose a personalized predictive-prescriptive framework that integrates interpretable machine learning with mixed-integer optimization. A supervised classifier trained on survey data predicts sleep quality, while SHAP-based feature attribution quantifies the influence of modifiable factors. These importance measures are incorporated into a mixed-integer optimization model that identifies minimal and feasible behavioral adjustments, while modelling resistance to change through a penalty mechanism. The framework achieves strong predictive performance, with a test F1-score of 0.9544 and an accuracy of 0.9366. Sensitivity and Pareto analyses reveal a clear trade-off between expected improvement and intervention intensity, with diminishing returns as additional changes are introduced. At the individual level, the model generates concise recommendations, often suggesting one or two high-impact behavioral adjustments and sometimes recommending no change when expected gains are minimal. By integrating prediction, explanation, and constrained optimization, this framework demonstrates how data-driven insights can be translated into structured and personalized decision support for sleep improvement.

AI.tldr: Personalized predictive-prescriptive framework combining interpretable ML with mixed-integer optimization to translate sleep quality predictions into actionable behavioral interventions.
AI.motivation: Most computational sleep studies focus on predictive risk identification rather than actionable intervention design. While ML models can accurately predict sleep outcomes, they rarely translate these predictions into practical intervention strategies.
AI.method: Integrated framework with supervised classifier for sleep quality prediction, SHAP-based feature attribution to quantify influence of modifiable factors, and mixed-integer optimization model that identifies minimal feasible behavioral adjustments with penalty mechanism for resistance to change.
AI.result: Achieved strong predictive performance (F1-score: 0.9544, accuracy: 0.9366). Sensitivity and Pareto analyses show trade-off between expected improvement and intervention intensity with diminishing returns. Individual recommendations typically suggest 1-2 high-impact adjustments or no change when gains are minimal.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates how data-driven insights can be translated into structured, personalized decision support for sleep improvement by integrating prediction, explanation, and constrained optimization.

title: GenLie: A Global-Enhanced Lie Detection Network under Sparsity and Semantic Interference
comment: Accepted to IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech and Signal Processing (ICASSP) 2026
summary: Video-based lie detection aims to identify deceptive behaviors from visual cues. Despite recent progress, its core challenge lies in learning sparse yet discriminative representations. Deceptive signals are typically subtle and short-lived, easily overwhelmed by redundant information, while individual and contextual variations introduce strong identity-related noise. To address this issue, we propose GenLie, a Global-Enhanced Lie Detection Network that performs local feature modeling under global supervision. Specifically, sparse and subtle deceptive cues are captured at the local level, while global supervision and optimization ensure robust and discriminative representations by suppressing identity-related noise. Experiments on three public datasets, covering both high- and low-stakes scenarios, show that GenLie consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods. Source code is available at https://github.com/AliasDictusZ1/GenLie.

AI.tldr: GenLie is a video-based lie detection network that uses local feature modeling with global supervision to capture subtle deceptive cues while suppressing identity-related noise.
AI.motivation: Current video-based lie detection struggles with learning sparse yet discriminative representations because deceptive signals are subtle and short-lived, easily overwhelmed by redundant information, while individual and contextual variations introduce strong identity-related noise.
AI.method: Proposes GenLie (Global-Enhanced Lie Detection Network) that performs local feature modeling under global supervision. Sparse deceptive cues are captured at local level, while global supervision and optimization suppress identity-related noise to ensure robust discriminative representations.
AI.result: Experiments on three public datasets covering both high- and low-stakes scenarios show that GenLie consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: GenLie effectively addresses the core challenge of video-based lie detection by combining local feature modeling with global supervision to capture subtle deceptive cues while suppressing identity noise, achieving superior performance across different scenarios.

title: Contrastive Reasoning Alignment: Reinforcement Learning from Hidden Representations
summary: We propose CRAFT, a red-teaming alignment framework that leverages model reasoning capabilities and hidden representations to improve robustness against jailbreak attacks. Unlike prior defenses that operate primarily at the output level, CRAFT aligns large reasoning models to generate safety-aware reasoning traces by explicitly optimizing objectives defined over the hidden state space. Methodologically, CRAFT integrates contrastive representation learning with reinforcement learning to separate safe and unsafe reasoning trajectories, yielding a latent-space geometry that supports robust, reasoning-level safety alignment. Theoretically, we show that incorporating latent-textual consistency into GRPO eliminates superficially aligned policies by ruling them out as local optima. Empirically, we evaluate CRAFT on multiple safety benchmarks using two strong reasoning models, Qwen3-4B-Thinking and R1-Distill-Llama-8B, where it consistently outperforms state-of-the-art defenses such as IPO and SafeKey. Notably, CRAFT delivers an average 79.0% improvement in reasoning safety and 87.7% improvement in final-response safety over the base models, demonstrating the effectiveness of hidden-space reasoning alignment.

AI.tldr: CRAFT is a red-teaming alignment framework that improves jailbreak defense by aligning large reasoning models to generate safety-aware reasoning traces through hidden state space optimization.
AI.motivation: Prior defenses operate primarily at output level, lacking robustness against jailbreak attacks. There's a need for deeper safety alignment that leverages model reasoning capabilities and hidden representations for more effective protection.
AI.method: CRAFT integrates contrastive representation learning with reinforcement learning to separate safe and unsafe reasoning trajectories in hidden state space. It optimizes objectives over hidden states and incorporates latent-textual consistency into GRPO to eliminate superficially aligned policies.
AI.result: CRAFT consistently outperforms state-of-the-art defenses (IPO, SafeKey) on multiple safety benchmarks using Qwen3-4B-Thinking and R1-Distill-Llama-8B models, achieving 79.0% improvement in reasoning safety and 87.7% improvement in final-response safety over base models.
AI.conclusion: Hidden-space reasoning alignment through CRAFT framework effectively improves robustness against jailbreak attacks by leveraging model reasoning capabilities and optimizing safety objectives in the latent representation space.

title: WiSLAT: A Simultaneous Device Localization and Target Tracking Method for Wi-Fi Systems
summary: It has been shown that the channel state information (CSI) of a Wi-Fi system can be exploited to localize Wi-Fi devices or track trajectory of a moving target. In the existing literature, both sensing tasks are treated separately and some prior information is usually requested, including the signal fingerprints, the locations of some anchor devices in the Wi-Fi system, and etc. In the proposed WiSLAT method, however, it is shown that both sensing tasks can assist each other, such that the request on prior system information can be eliminated. Particularly, in a Wi-Fi system with an access point (AP) and at least three stations, where the locations of the stations are unknown, the WiSLAT is designed to detect the Doppler frequencies of the downlink CSI at the stations, such that their locations and the trajectory of the target with respect to the AP can be inferred. The joint detection can be conducted by searching the optimal stations' locations and target's trajectory, such that their corresponding Doppler frequencies fit the observed ones best. Due to the tremendous non-convex search space, a low-complexity sub-optimal algorithm integrating alternate optimization, extended Kalman filter and density-based clustering is proposed in WiSLAT. Experiments conducted in indoor environments demonstrate the effectiveness of WiSLAT, achieving a median trajectory-tracking error of 0.68 m.

AI.tldr: WiSLAT: A method that jointly performs Wi-Fi device localization and target trajectory tracking using CSI without prior information, achieving 0.68m median tracking error.
AI.motivation: Existing Wi-Fi sensing approaches treat device localization and target tracking as separate tasks requiring prior information like signal fingerprints or anchor device locations. This limits practical deployment and increases setup complexity.
AI.method: WiSLAT uses Doppler frequencies from downlink CSI at stations to jointly infer unknown station locations and target trajectory relative to the AP. It employs alternate optimization, extended Kalman filter, and density-based clustering to handle the non-convex search space efficiently.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation in indoor environments shows WiSLAT achieves a median trajectory-tracking error of 0.68 meters, demonstrating effective joint localization and tracking without prior information.
AI.conclusion: WiSLAT successfully eliminates the need for prior system information by enabling device localization and target tracking to assist each other, providing a practical solution for Wi-Fi sensing applications.

title: Influence of Gripper Design on Human Demonstration Quality for Robot Learning
comment: To be published in proceedings of 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics & Automation
summary: Opening sterile medical packaging is routine for healthcare workers but remains challenging for robots. Learning from demonstration enables robots to acquire manipulation skills directly from humans, and handheld gripper tools such as the Universal Manipulation Interface (UMI) offer a pathway for efficient data collection. However, the effectiveness of these tools depends heavily on their usability. We evaluated UMI in demonstrating a bandage opening task, a common manipulation task in hospital settings, by testing three conditions: distributed load grippers, concentrated load grippers, and bare hands. Eight participants performed timed trials, with task performance assessed by success rate, completion time, and damage, alongside perceived workload using the NASA-TLX questionnaire. Concentrated load grippers improved performance relative to distributed load grippers but remained substantially slower and less effective than hands. These results underscore the importance of ergonomic and mechanical refinements in handheld grippers to reduce user burden and improve demonstration quality, especially for applications in healthcare robotics.

AI.tldr: Handheld gripper tools like UMI for robot learning from demonstration need usability improvements for healthcare tasks like bandage opening, as concentrated load grippers perform better than distributed ones but still worse than bare hands.
AI.motivation: Robots need to learn manipulation skills for healthcare tasks like opening sterile medical packaging, and handheld gripper tools offer efficient data collection for learning from demonstration, but their usability needs evaluation.
AI.method: Evaluated UMI gripper for bandage opening task with 8 participants in three conditions: distributed load grippers, concentrated load grippers, and bare hands. Measured success rate, completion time, damage, and NASA-TLX workload.
AI.result: Concentrated load grippers improved performance over distributed load grippers but were still substantially slower and less effective than bare hands. All gripper conditions showed higher perceived workload than hands.
AI.conclusion: Handheld gripper tools need ergonomic and mechanical refinements to reduce user burden and improve demonstration quality for healthcare robotics applications, as current designs remain inferior to human hands.

title: Minimum-Action Learning: Energy-Constrained Symbolic Model Selection for Physical Law Identification from Noisy Data
summary: Identifying physical laws from noisy observational data is a central challenge in scientific machine learning. We present Minimum-Action Learning (MAL), a framework that selects symbolic force laws from a pre-specified basis library by minimizing a Triple-Action functional combining trajectory reconstruction, architectural sparsity, and energy-conservation enforcement. A wide-stencil acceleration-matching technique reduces noise variance by 10,000x, transforming an intractable problem (SNR ~0.02) into a learnable one (SNR ~1.6); this preprocessing is the critical enabler shared by all methods tested, including SINDy variants. On two benchmarks -- Kepler gravity and Hooke's law -- MAL recovers the correct force law with Kepler exponent p = 3.01 +/- 0.01 at ~0.07 kWh (40% reduction vs. prediction-error-only baselines). The raw correct-basis rate is 40% for Kepler and 90% for Hooke; an energy-conservation-based criterion discriminates the true force law in all cases, yielding 100% pipeline-level identification. Basis library sensitivity experiments show that near-confounders degrade selection (20% with added r^{-2.5} and r^{-1.5}), while distant additions are harmless, and the conservation diagnostic remains informative even when the correct basis is absent. Direct comparison with noise-robust SINDy variants, Hamiltonian Neural Networks, and Lagrangian Neural Networks confirms MAL's distinct niche: interpretable, energy-constrained model selection that combines symbolic basis identification with dynamical rollout validation.

AI.tldr: MAL framework identifies symbolic force laws from noisy data by minimizing a Triple-Action functional combining trajectory reconstruction, sparsity, and energy conservation, achieving 100% pipeline-level identification with noise reduction preprocessing.
AI.motivation: Identifying physical laws from noisy observational data is a central challenge in scientific machine learning, requiring methods that can handle low signal-to-noise ratios while maintaining interpretability and physical consistency.
AI.method: Minimum-Action Learning (MAL) selects symbolic force laws from a basis library by minimizing a Triple-Action functional that combines trajectory reconstruction, architectural sparsity, and energy-conservation enforcement. Uses wide-stencil acceleration-matching for 10,000x noise reduction preprocessing.
AI.result: Recovers Kepler gravity with exponent p = 3.01 ± 0.01 using ~0.07 kWh (40% reduction vs baselines). Raw correct-basis rate: 40% for Kepler, 90% for Hooke's law. Energy-conservation criterion yields 100% pipeline-level identification. Outperforms SINDy variants, Hamiltonian Neural Networks, and Lagrangian Neural Networks.
AI.conclusion: MAL provides interpretable, energy-constrained model selection combining symbolic basis identification with dynamical rollout validation, offering a distinct niche for scientific machine learning applications requiring physical consistency and noise robustness.

title: TDMM-LM: Bridging Facial Understanding and Animation via Language Models
summary: Text-guided human body animation has advanced rapidly, yet facial animation lags due to the scarcity of well-annotated, text-paired facial corpora. To close this gap, we leverage foundation generative models to synthesize a large, balanced corpus of facial behavior. We design prompts suite covering emotions and head motions, generate about 80 hours of facial videos with multiple generators, and fit per-frame 3D facial parameters, yielding large-scale (prompt and parameter) pairs for training. Building on this dataset, we probe language models for bidirectional competence over facial motion via two complementary tasks: (1) Motion2Language: given a sequence of 3D facial parameters, the model produces natural-language descriptions capturing content, style, and dynamics; and (2) Language2Motion: given a prompt, the model synthesizes the corresponding sequence of 3D facial parameters via quantized motion tokens for downstream animation. Extensive experiments show that in this setting language models can both interpret and synthesize facial motion with strong generalization. To best of our knowledge, this is the first work to cast facial-parameter modeling as a language problem, establishing a unified path for text-conditioned facial animation and motion understanding.

AI.tldr: This paper addresses the gap in text-guided facial animation by creating a large synthetic facial behavior dataset using foundation models, then training language models for bidirectional facial motion understanding and generation.
AI.motivation: While text-guided human body animation has advanced, facial animation lags behind due to the scarcity of well-annotated, text-paired facial corpora. There's a need to close this gap and enable better text-conditioned facial animation.
AI.method: 1) Generate a large synthetic facial behavior dataset using foundation generative models with emotion and head motion prompts, producing ~80 hours of facial videos; 2) Fit per-frame 3D facial parameters to create (prompt, parameter) pairs; 3) Train language models for bidirectional tasks: Motion2Language (describing facial motion from parameters) and Language2Motion (generating facial parameters from text via quantized motion tokens).
AI.result: Language models demonstrate strong generalization in both interpreting and synthesizing facial motion. The approach establishes that facial-parameter modeling can be effectively cast as a language problem, enabling unified text-conditioned facial animation and motion understanding.
AI.conclusion: This work pioneers casting facial-parameter modeling as a language problem, creating the first unified framework for bidirectional facial motion understanding and generation through language models, bridging the gap in text-guided facial animation.

title: InfoDensity: Rewarding Information-Dense Traces for Efficient Reasoning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) with extended reasoning capabilities often generate verbose and redundant reasoning traces, incurring unnecessary computational cost. While existing reinforcement learning approaches address this by optimizing final response length, they neglect the quality of intermediate reasoning steps, leaving models vulnerable to reward hacking. We argue that verbosity is not merely a length problem, but a symptom of poor intermediate reasoning quality. To investigate this, we conduct an empirical study tracking the conditional entropy of the answer distribution across reasoning steps. We find that high-quality reasoning traces exhibit two consistent properties: low uncertainty convergence and monotonic progress. These findings suggest that high-quality reasoning traces are informationally dense, that is, each step contributes meaningful entropy reduction relative to the total reasoning length. Motivated by this, we propose InfoDensity, a reward framework for RL training that combines an AUC-based reward and a monotonicity reward as a unified measure of reasoning quality, weighted by a length scaling term that favors achieving equivalent quality more concisely. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that InfoDensity matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines in accuracy while significantly reducing token usage, achieving a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off.

AI.tldr: InfoDensity: A reward framework for RL training that optimizes reasoning quality through entropy reduction and monotonic progress, reducing verbosity while maintaining accuracy in LLM reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: LLMs generate verbose and redundant reasoning traces with unnecessary computational costs. Existing RL approaches only optimize final response length, neglecting intermediate reasoning quality and making models vulnerable to reward hacking. The authors argue verbosity is a symptom of poor intermediate reasoning quality rather than just a length problem.
AI.method: 1) Empirical study tracking conditional entropy of answer distribution across reasoning steps, revealing high-quality reasoning exhibits low uncertainty convergence and monotonic progress. 2) Proposed InfoDensity reward framework combining AUC-based reward (measuring entropy reduction) and monotonicity reward as unified reasoning quality measure, weighted by length scaling term favoring concise quality achievement.
AI.result: Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show InfoDensity matches or surpasses state-of-the-art baselines in accuracy while significantly reducing token usage, achieving strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off.
AI.conclusion: High-quality reasoning traces are informationally dense, with each step contributing meaningful entropy reduction. InfoDensity effectively optimizes reasoning quality through entropy-based metrics, addressing verbosity as a quality issue rather than just a length problem.

title: Near-Field NLOS Localization via Position-Unknown HRIS:From Self-Localization to Target Positioning
summary: Current reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-aided near-field (NF) localization methods assume the RIS position is known a priori, and it has limited their practical applicability. This paper applies a hybrid RIS (HRIS) at an unknown position to locate non-line-of-sight (NLOS) NF targets. To this end, we first propose a two-stage gridless localization framework for achieving HRIS self-localization, and then determine the positions of the NF targets. In the first stage, we use the NF Fresnel approximation to convert the signal model into a virtual far-field model through delay-based cross-correlation of centrally symmetric HRIS elements. Such a conversion will naturally extend the aperture of the virtual array. A single-snapshot decoupled atomic norm minimization (DANM) algorithm is then proposed to locate an NF target relative to the HRIS, which includes a two-dimensional (2-D) direction of arrival (DOA) estimation with automatic pairing, the multiple signal classification (MUSIC) method for range estimation, and a total least squares (TLS) method to eliminate the Fresnel approximation error. In the second stage, we leverage the unique capability of HRIS in simultaneous sensing and reflection to estimate the HRIS-to-base station (BS) direction vectors using atomic norm minimization (ANM), and derive the three-dimensional (3-D) HRIS position with two BSs via the least squares (LS)-based geometric triangulation. Furthermore, we propose a semidefinite relaxation (SDR)-based HRIS phase optimization method to enhance the received signal power at the BSs, thereby improving the HRIS localization accuracy, which, in turn, enhances NF target positionings. The Cramer-Rao bound (CRB) for the NF target parameters and the position error bound (PEB) for the HRIS coordinates are derived as performance benchmarks.
